text.title: Support Processes, Pattern Detection, and Product Feedback Loop
text.summary: Ticket categorization, resolution tracking metrics, product feedback loop from support data, communication tips, and agent guardrails.

section.title: Ticket Categorization

rule.statement: Categorize every ticket as: bug-blocking (critical), bug-non-blocking (high), how-to (medium), feature-request (low), general-feedback (low). Track volume by category weekly.
rule.rationale: Category distribution is a leading product signal. How-to tickets above 20% of volume for a specific feature indicate a UX problem, not a documentation gap.

rule.statement: If how-to tickets for a specific feature exceed 20% of volume, that feature needs UX work. More documentation for a confusing flow does not fix the confusion.
rule.rationale: Documentation treats the symptom. Fixing the UX removes the root cause and permanently reduces support load for that flow.

section.reference: Count tickets by category from CSV export and output weekly volume summary

list.item: First response time target: under 4 hours (shows attentiveness)
First contact resolution target: above 70% (efficiency signal)
Tickets per customer over time: should be decreasing (product improvement signal)

rule.statement: Never let an agent send a response with a specific commit, deadline, or "we will fix this by [date]" — those require human sign-off on feasibility.
rule.rationale: Commitment to a deadline without verifying engineering capacity creates a new customer expectation that, if missed, causes more churn than the original bug.

list.item: Bug report: "Thanks for reporting. I'll look into this." — not "That shouldn't happen"
Feature request: "Great idea. I've added it to our list." — not "We won't build that"
Angry customer: "I understand the frustration. Let me fix this." — not "Calm down"
User error: "Easy to miss. Here's how it works..." — not "You did it wrong"

section.title: Agent Guardrails

rule.statement: Agents must not mark tickets as resolved automatically. Only draft the response; resolution confirmation requires human action.
rule.rationale: A ticket marked resolved without customer confirmation produces a false CSAT signal and may leave the customer's issue unresolved, increasing churn risk.

rule.statement: Inject tone guidelines explicitly: "friendly, empathetic, first-person" for consumer products. Agents default to neutral-formal, which underperforms for consumer support.
rule.rationale: Tone mismatch in support responses reduces CSAT and increases escalations. Consumer customers expect warmth; enterprise customers expect professionalism. Both require explicit guidance.

section.reference: Weekly support review template with volume, performance metrics, top issues, and action items
